Helleborine, a type of leafless orchid, is a non-photosynthetic, parasitic plant that obtains its nutrients by connecting to the roots of other plants. The health benefits of Helleborine are largely unknown, but some species of the genus have been used in traditional medicine for their potential antiviral, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. The bioactive constituents of Helleborine include iridoid glycosides, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which may contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Helleborine is often prepared as a tincture or infusion, and has been used to treat various health issues, although its effectiveness and safety have not been extensively scientifically studied.

The species Helleborine squamellosa is further classified as Epipactis squamellosa, and it is often confused with other species within the Epipactis genus.
